2 20 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, 38 Finland. 39 21 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology Clinical Trials Unit, Helsinki University 40 Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 41 22 Lead contact. 42 Abstract 43 The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, 44 yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly 45 understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC 46 from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. 47 Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically 48 heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the 49 tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we 50 identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. 51 Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic 52 datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune 53 activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived 54 immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with 55 increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy.

Together, our atlas offers 56 novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification 57 in HGSC. 58 INTRODUCTION 59 The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, 60 immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 1. The 61 continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is 62 fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and 63 therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most 64 prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 2,3, characterized by high genomic 65 instability 4,5 and significant tumor heterogeneity 6. This heterogeneity is evident in 66 various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME 67 cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 7. The genetic 68 diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with 69 a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 70 BRCA1/2 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher 71

3 lymphocyte infiltration 8,9. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) 72 tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 10 and unique responses to 73 combinations with immunotherapies 11,12. Moreover, we and others have shown that 74 exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by 75 enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 13–15, resulting in 76 spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 16. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex 77 relationships between clinical and molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor 78 ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment 79 strategies for patients with HGSC. 80 We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single 81 cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and 82 multiomic molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and 83 mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial 84 TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that 85 associate with patient outcomes.

We employed a custom machine learning tool, 86 CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate 87 single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. 88 We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression 89 specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of 90 immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune 91 hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of 92 clinicomolecular profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune 93 cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for 94 targeted interventions in HGSC. 95 RESULTS 96 Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors 97 To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical 98 prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic profiling including 99 targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA 100 methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 101 advanced HGSCs (Fig. 1a).

The intricate interplay between immune co-279 infiltration, spatial organization, and clinicomolecular characteristics suggests a 280 coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the necessity of 281 integrative approaches to uncover the key features associated with the observed 282 spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME. 283 CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor 284 ecosystem features 285 To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and 286 prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom 287 forest), a robust and generalized machine learning (ML) framework (Fig. 3a). The 288 primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-289 dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. CEFIIRA 290 robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical 291 features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation 292

10 in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent 293 overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and 294 balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a 295 prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a 296 sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical 297 features using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the 298 previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus 299 estimation. Additionally, our versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate 300 multidimensional data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient 301 and molecular profiles (Fig. 3a). 302 We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the 4,644,485 single 303 cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (Fig. 1e, i; S. This analysis revealed a higher fraction of 377 MHCII+ tumor cells associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, 378 and M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (Fig. 4a). To corroborate these findings, we 379 tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer cells and the estimated 380 proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see 381 Methods). We found that tumors with high MHCII expression in cancer cells were 382 significantly enriched with T cells and M1-like macrophages (S. Fig. 4a). In contrast, 383 tumors with high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-384 like macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for anti-385 tumor responses (S. Fig. 4a). Overall, these results demonstrate that HGSC tumors 386 with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more immune infiltration in the 387 TME. 388

13 To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and underlying 389 transcriptional programs, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both from PDS (N=29), and 390 IDS (n=22), from 29 patients using single-cell RNA sequencing23 (scRNA-seq) (Fig. 391 4b). The dataset comprised 136,452 cells, which included 25,091 epithelial/cancer 392 cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal cells, and 97,409 immune cells, annotated using 393 canonical phenotypic markers as described previously 23 (see Methods). 17 Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR 516 diversity and enhanced immunotherapy response in vitro 517 Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when antigen-518 loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous misfolded proteins 519 46. To explore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with specific antigen 520 presentation, we focused on previously identified antigens detected via HLA 521 ligandome analysis in HGSC 47 (Fig. 6a). We found that the deconvoluted expression 522 of MHCII in cancer cells is indeed associated with the expression of most antigen-523 encoding genes in the TCGA dataset (Fig. 6b). Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying 565 phenotypic compositions.

These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor 566 cell phenotypic profiles and clinicomolecular groups, underscoring the role of 567 molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the 568 two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated with cancer 569 cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while the immune-cold TSI 570 displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 571 Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional 572 single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through 573 spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that 574 MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate 575 immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with 576 enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and 577 antigen-presenting cells.

Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize 578 MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune 579 surveillance 48,49, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T 580

While IFN expression in 581 tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests 582 it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 51. Through a robust spatial 583 TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within 584 immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. 585 Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced 586 CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively 587 present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII 588 and IFN-γ expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic 589 MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and 590 antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in Fig. 7, 591 support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ 592 T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated 593 with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 594 MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary 595 pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 52.

In 596 HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs undergoing 597 whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with poor prognosis 53. 598 Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved 599 response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-600 negative breast cancer 41,50,54,55. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-601 L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated 602 only modest benefits 56. In contrast, our in vitro analysis of immunotherapy responses 603 in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might 604 enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic activity of 605 CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. In addition, our study revealed that other 606 immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in 607 tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical 608 exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients 609 with HGSC 16.

While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there 625 is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have 626 matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning 627 models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further 628 improve predictive performance.
